body.label: thm:even_or_next_even
body.claim: For every natural number n, either n is even or n+1 is even.

base_case_proof.claim: Either 0 is even or 0+1 is even.
base_case_proof.text: Base case n=0: 0 is even, so either 0 is even or 1 is even.

proof.claim: Either n+1 is even or n+2 is even.
proof.text: Case 1: n is even. Then n=2k for some k, so n+2=2(k+1) is even. Thus the right disjunct of the goal, n+2 is even, holds.

proof.claim: Either n+1 is even or n+2 is even.
proof.text: Case 2: n+1 is even. This is exactly the left disjunct of the goal, so either n+1 is even or n+2 is even.

We prove this by induction on n. For n=0, n is even. For the induction step, assume that either n is even or n+1 is even. We split into cases. If n is even, then n=2k for some natural number k, hence n+2=2(k+1) is even, so the right side of the desired disjunction holds. If n+1 is even, then the left side of the desired disjunction holds.
